questions.question: In `fn longest<'a>(s1: &'a str, s2: &'a str) -> &'a str`, what does `'a` mean?
options: A lifetime parameter: 'for some scope 'a, s1, s2, and the return reference are all valid'. The compiler picks 'a as the shorter of the input lifetimes.
questions.explanation: Lifetimes are generic parameters over scopes. The compiler chooses 'a to be the intersection of the input lifetimes — the period when ALL relevant references are valid. The returned reference's lifetime is then guaranteed not to outlive any input.

questions.question: What does RAII stand for and what does it guarantee?
options: Resource Acquisition Is Initialization: the constructor acquires a resource; the destructor releases it. Scope exit always runs the destructor, including on exception unwinding.
questions.explanation: RAII binds resource lifetime to object scope. C++ destructors run automatically; Rust's `Drop` trait is the same idea. Exception-safe code follows: even if a function throws midway, every constructed local has its destructor called during stack unwinding, releasing files, locks, memory.

questions.question: When are Rust lifetime annotations REQUIRED on a function?
options: When the lifetime elision rules can't unambiguously infer them: multiple input references and an output reference that doesn't trivially correspond to one input.
questions.explanation: Elision rules: (1) each input ref gets its own lifetime; (2) one input lifetime → output gets it; (3) `&self` present → output gets `self`'s lifetime. When these don't unambiguously assign an output lifetime — e.g., `fn f(a: &str, b: &str) -> &str` — the compiler forces you to annotate.

questions.question: What does C++'s `std::unique_ptr<T>` provide?
options: RAII wrapper for sole heap ownership: deletes the underlying object in its destructor. No copy; only move. Replacement for naked `new`/`delete`.
questions.explanation: unique_ptr is move-only. Exactly one unique_ptr owns the resource; transferring ownership is explicit (`std::move`). On destruction, it calls `delete` on the pointee. Modern C++ uses unique_ptr (or shared_ptr) instead of naked new/delete — eliminating leaks and double-free bugs in well-written code.

questions.question: Rust's `Box<T>`, `Rc<T>`, and `Arc<T>` differ how?
options: Box: sole ownership (= unique_ptr). Rc: reference-counted shared ownership (single-threaded; non-atomic refcount). Arc: same but with atomic refcount, safe across threads.
questions.explanation: Box<T>: one owner; minimal overhead. Rc<T>: cheap refcount but not Send (single-threaded only). Arc<T>: atomic refcount (~10× slower than Rc) but thread-safe; Send + Sync. Choose by use-case: Box almost always; Rc for shared graphs in a single thread; Arc for cross-thread sharing.

questions.question: What is Rust's `'static` lifetime?
options: A lifetime that lasts for the entire program. String literals have it; so do `const` and `static` items. Don't reach for `'static` to silence the borrow checker — it's a real claim.
questions.explanation: `'static` means 'lives forever.' Genuinely-static things (string literals, leaked memory, global statics) have this lifetime. Putting `'static` everywhere defeats the type system — you'd often need to `clone` or `Box::leak` the data. Use it only when the value really lives that long.
